title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: This PR introduces coupon-discount support but contains two correctness bugs severe enough to make the feature entirely broken in production: the `discount_amount` field is always 0 due to a self-referential subtraction, and fixed-amount coupons can produce negative charge amounts. There is also a critical PCI/PII violation where the full request body (including card data) is logged to stdout. Beyond those blockers, the code has a silently-swallowed exception, poor naming throughout, debug logs left in, missing schema validation for the new field, commented-out dead code, and no unit tests.

findings.title: Full request body logged — card data / PII / PCI violation
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG charge body:', JSON.stringify(theData))` dumps the entire request body, which almost certainly contains raw card numbers, CVVs, and other cardholder data to stdout/log aggregators. This is a PCI-DSS violation and a serious data-exposure risk. Remove this line immediately. If request tracing is needed, log only a safe subset (e.g., `customerId`, masked card last-4) through a structured logger, never the raw body.

findings.title: `discount_amount` is always 0 — payload.amount mutated before subtraction
findings.message: Line 24 sets `payload.amount = finalAmount` (the already-discounted value). Line 36 then computes `payload.amount - finalAmount`, which is `finalAmount - finalAmount = 0` for every request. The discount amount is therefore never surfaced to the client. Fix by preserving the original amount before applying the coupon: `const originalAmount = payload.amount;` then `response.discount_amount = originalAmount - finalAmount;`. The `payload.amount = finalAmount` mutation on line 24 is also unnecessary and confusing — remove it and rely solely on `finalAmount`.

findings.title: FixedCouponStrategy can produce negative charge amounts
findings.message: `return amount - this.amt;` has no lower-bound guard. If a user charges $3 and applies `FIVEOFF` (500 cents), the result is -200. A negative amount will likely cause the payment gateway to reject the request or, worse, issue a refund. Add `return Math.max(0, amount - this.amt);` at minimum, or throw/reject if the coupon exceeds the charge amount so the caller can return a proper 422 error.

findings.title: Coupon errors silently swallowed — payment proceeds at wrong amount
findings.message: The inner `try/catch` swallows all exceptions from `doStuff` with only a `// TODO` comment. If an unexpected error occurs (e.g., the factory throws instead of returning null), `finalAmount` stays as `payload.amount`, the charge goes through at the undiscounted amount, but the client sees no error. Either let coupon errors propagate (and handle them explicitly with a 422 or 500), or at minimum log the error so it is observable.

findings.title: `couponCode` extracted from raw body before schema validation
findings.message: `couponCode` is read directly from `req.body` before Zod parses and validates the request. This means it bypasses any type coercion, sanitisation, or allow-listing the schema could enforce. Add `couponCode` to `chargeSchema` as an optional string field and read it from `payload` after parsing, keeping validation in one place.

findings.title: Coupon fields always added to response, even when no coupon was used
findings.message: `response.coupon_code` and `response.discount_amount` are unconditionally set on every response. When no coupon code is provided, `discount_amount` will be 0 and `coupon_code` will be `undefined` (serialised as absent in JSON, but the 0 leaks). Only add these fields when a coupon was actually applied: `if (couponCode && finalAmount !== originalAmount) { response.discount_amount = …; response.coupon_code = couponCode; }`.

findings.title: `const response: any = transaction` defeats TypeScript type safety
findings.message: Casting to `any` removes all compile-time guarantees on the response shape. Define a proper response type that extends the transaction type with optional `discount_amount: number` and `coupon_code: string` fields, or construct a new plain object rather than mutating the domain entity.

findings.title: Public function named `doStuff` — meaningless name
findings.message: `doStuff` is exported and referenced by the controller. The name communicates nothing about its purpose. Rename to something descriptive, e.g. `applyCoupon(amount: number, couponCode: string | undefined): number`. The variable names `tmp` and `result2` inside the function have the same problem.

findings.title: Unknown coupon code logged to console — information leakage and debug noise
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG: unknown coupon code:', couponCode)` echoes untrusted user input directly to logs. This leaks probe attempts (attackers iterating through potential codes can confirm non-matches via log correlation), pollutes log aggregators, and is not the right tool for observability. Remove the `DEBUG:` prefix and the raw code value; instead, increment a metric counter or log at a structured `warn` level with a safe event name.

findings.title: No unit tests for coupon math — acknowledged as TODO in the PR
findings.message: The PR description explicitly defers unit tests to a follow-up. Given that the discount calculation is already broken (see `discount_amount` bug) and edge cases like negative amounts are unhandled, tests should gate this merge, not follow it. At minimum: test each coupon code returns the correct discounted amount, test that a fixed discount on an amount smaller than the discount clamps to 0, and test that an unknown code leaves the amount unchanged.

findings.title: Commented-out dead code should not be merged
findings.message: The `FREESHIP` block (`// else if (code == 'FREESHIP') { … }`) is commented out. Dead code adds noise for reviewers and future maintainers. Remove it; if the feature is planned, track it in the issue tracker instead.

findings.title: Strategy pattern is over-engineered for 6 static string-to-discount mappings
findings.message: Three classes (`AbstractCouponStrategy`, `PercentageCouponStrategy`, `FixedCouponStrategy`) and a factory class (`CouponHandlerFactory`) to look up a discount in what is effectively a static map adds significant indirection for no current benefit. A simple record/map of `{ [code: string]: (amount: number) => number }` would be easier to read and extend. The abstract class can also be replaced by an interface, since TypeScript interfaces are structurally typed and do not impose a class hierarchy.

findings.title: `var` used instead of `const`/`let` in TypeScript code
findings.message: `var x = amount * (1 - this.p);` (line 16) and `var tmp = CouponHandlerFactory.build(couponCode);` (line 58) use `var`, which has function-level scoping and is considered legacy in modern JavaScript/TypeScript. Replace with `const` (preferred where the binding is not reassigned) or `let`.

findings.title: Loose equality `==` used instead of strict `===` throughout factory
findings.message: All comparisons in `CouponHandlerFactory.build` use `==` (e.g., `code == 'WELCOME10'`). In TypeScript, `===` is the idiomatic choice and avoids unexpected type-coercion behaviour. The same applies to `tmp == null` on line 59 (though `== null` covering both `null` and `undefined` is an accepted idiom — still worth being intentional about it and adding a comment if kept).
